text: The video shows the capillary network on the peritoneum moving laterally with respiration. The dotted line indicates the location of the linea semilunaris, with the peritoneum densely adhered below the rectus abdominis, serving as a marker.

The surgeon performs a pinch test at the level of the umbilicus, approximately five to seven centimeters proximally and distally, to assess the pinchability of the peritoneum and determine if a transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) approach is feasible.

The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris on the opposite side using anatomical landmarks, then performs a pinch test on the peritoneum to assess its separability before placing the peritoneal incision for a ventral tap meshplasty.
The surgeon makes an incision along the border of the linea semilunaris, extending approximately seven to ten centimeters above and below the umbilicus, preparing the area for mesh placement.

The surgeon encounters loose and flimsy areolar tissue in the surgical plane, which is easily separated with swiping movements. In this patient, there is also some fleshy areolar tissue with sizable vessels requiring an energy source for division. The surgeon focuses distally, moving lower to complete the incision and create the surgical space.
